facts.context: Mass-society theory suggests that:
facts.truth: the media manipulate 'the masses' as vulnerable, passive consumers
distractors: the content of the media is determined by market forces
the subordinate classes are dominated by the ideology of the ruling class
audiences make selective interpretations of media messages

facts.context: The ecological approach to urban sociology involved studying:
facts.truth: how social groups colonized different areas of the city and competed for resources
distractors: the forms of wildlife and natural habitats that could be found on the edges of the city
the way in which people organized collective protests about environmental issues
how men and women used the city's public spaces differently

facts.context: Becker proclaimed that cannabis use was:
facts.truth: learned gradually through the social processes of a deviant career
distractors: increasing throughout all sections of youth in the 1970s
a dangerous pastime that young people should not be allowed to pursue
not as enjoyable as other sociologists had suggested

facts.context: The middle classes that developed over the nineteenth century were:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: an urban set, involved in civic bodies and voluntary associations
too diverse to have a strong sense of class consciousness
often involved in 'white collar' work

facts.context: Technological forms of surveillance have made it easier to:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: access and exchange 'private' information about consumers
reduce prison overcrowding by the use of electronic tagging
monitor employees' activities at work

facts.context: The concept of gemeinschaft developed by Ferdinand Tönnies describes basically the same relational characteristics as
facts.truth: mechanical solidarity
distractors: organic solidarity
gesellschaft
anomie

facts.context: There was a growth in income inequality in the 1980s because:
facts.truth: growing unemployment made more people dependent on welfare benefits
distractors: rates of income tax increased equally for all occupational groups
there were more professional jobs available but not enough people to fill them
the price of consumer goods rose at a higher rate than earnings

facts.context: If a deviant act is 'normalized', it is:
facts.truth: seen a temporary aberration from an otherwise 'normal' character
distractors: recognised as breaking an important norm of behaviour
the first step in establishing a deviant career
attributed to the person's genetic or anatomical make up

facts.context: In the debate about the 'managerial revolution', John Scott (1997) argued that:
facts.truth: owners still exercised control through groups of shareholders and interlocking directorships
distractors: ownership and management were becoming increasingly separate functions
the techniques of scientific management were overly bureaucratic and inefficient
managers were becoming dissatisfied with their working conditions and planned to overthrow the owners of businesses

facts.context: Marxist feminists explain patriarchy in terms of:
facts.truth: women's domestic labour being exploited by the capitalist economy
distractors: a lack of equal rights and opportunities for men and women
sex classes, through which men oppress women economically, politically and sexually
the dual systems of capitalism and male domination

facts.context: Which of the following was not defined by Giddens (1998) as part of New Labour's 'third way':
facts.truth: putting an end to privatization
distractors: the democratization of the family
positive welfare: 'a hand up, not a hand-out'
the strengthening of civil society

facts.context: Anti-psychiatrists like Laing & Esterson believed that:
facts.truth: Behaviour that seemed bizarre could be seen as a reasonable response to disturbed patterns of family interaction
distractors: Psychiatry was an out-moded form of treatment and should be replaced by alternative therapies like aromatherapy and kinesiology
Mental illnesses were biological in origin, so there was no point in studying 'the mind' as experienced by the patient
Psychiatrists were agents of capitalism who tried to instill ruling class ideology into their patients

facts.context: Butler & Stokes (1969) suggested that working class support for the Labour Party arose from:
facts.truth: socialization into working class families and communities
distractors: rational calculation of self-interest
issue-based concerns about culture and lifestyle
false consciousness and the acceptance of hegemonic values

facts.context: In contemporary societies, social institutions are:
facts.truth: highly specialized, interrelated sets of social practices
distractors: disorganized social relations in a postmodern world
virtual communities in cyberspace
no longer relevant to sociology

facts.context: The term 'emotion work' (Hochschild 1983) refers to:
facts.truth: the techniques used to consciously manage and present emotions that are deemed appropriate for public settings of interaction
distractors: the work done by psychologists, counselors and therapists to help clients with mental and emotional problems
experiences such as grieving and falling in love, which demand a lot of emotional energy from a person
the way in which increasing levels of risk and anxiety have made us think more reflexively about our self-identities

facts.context: When sociologists study the structure of layers in society and people's movement between them, they call this:
facts.truth: social stratification
distractors: social control
social conflict
social solidarity

facts.context: The four ideal types of social action that Weber identified were as follows: instrumentally-rational, value-rational, traditional, and...
facts.truth: affectual
distractors: affective
effective
infected

facts.context: An ecclesia is:
facts.truth: a religious organization that claims total spiritual authority over its members
distractors: a church organized around voluntary rather than compulsory membership
a sect or cult with a very small following
a hierarchy of priests or other spiritual leaders

facts.context: After slavery was abolished in the USA and British Empire, policy makers claimed that former slaves migrating to these cities would experience:
facts.truth: assimilation
distractors: adaptation
discrimination
compensation

facts.context: Sutherland's study of the 'professional thief' suggested that:
facts.truth: people are socialized into a life of crime by associating with others who define it in positive terms
distractors: the majority of crime is committed by middle class people in professional occupations
those who were arrested and charged with theft did not define themselves as thieves
the most dangerous criminals on the street were those who were highly skilled thieves

facts.context: Which of the following policies did the New Labour government not pursue?
facts.truth: supporting LEAs that appeared to be failing
distractors: setting and streaming pupils by ability
parental choice of school
state regulation and control of the curriculum

facts.context: Stone's research suggests that prior to industrialization, the nuclear family:
facts.truth: had begun to emerge through the separation of work and home life
distractors: did not exist in any form
had begun to disappear, as extended networks of kin became more important
was simply another institution of patriarchal control

facts.context: Statistics about rural, suburban, and urban patterns of development and mobility are almost always reported based on a geographical area labeled as Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSA). What constitutes an MSA?
facts.truth: It is composed of at least one central city with a population of at least 50,000 and it surrounds densely urbanized counties.
distractors: It includes only the 10 largest cities in the United States.
It is an area that experiences strong storms at least 50 percent of the year.
It is a large city with a population of at least 1 million and is immediately surrounded by suburbs.

facts.context: Mosca and Pareto identified the ruling elite as:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: a minority group who fill all the top positions of political authority
a coalition of social forces with specific skills and abilities
a group who circulate between high status positions and exclude others

facts.context: Bourdieu attributed the reproduction of class to:
facts.truth: cultural capital
distractors: cults of the capital
capital culture
culpable capture

facts.context: Secularization involves the two related ideas of:
facts.truth: disengagement and disenchantment
distractors: disappointment and disproportion
disbelief and disintegration
distribution and distillation

facts.context: The general fertility rate fell between 1964 and 2000 because of:
facts.truth: increases in the age of marriage and the age at which childbearing begins
distractors: there being fewer women of childbearing age than there were in the first half of the century
errors in the measurement of birth rates and production of statistics
the risk of diseases caused by poverty, poor housing and bad sanitation

facts.context: Howard's idea of the garden city consisted of:
facts.truth: six small towns surrounded by green belts and linked to a larger central city
distractors: planning cities to have plenty of trees, flowers and public gardens
each privately owned house to have its own front and back garden
tower blocks to displace the population vertically and leave most of the land available as green open spaces

facts.context: The 'third age' of the life course is said to involve:
facts.truth: active non-work and independence after retirement
distractors: full time employment, family-building and adult responsibility
illness, isolation and increasing dependence on others
the transition from education to work, and distinctive youth cultures

facts.context: Weber (1919) said that the state's monopoly of the use of force was legitimated by:
facts.truth: rational-legal authority
distractors: charismatic authority
traditional authority
value-rational authority

facts.context: Sullivan's (2000) study suggested that the proportion of housework men did was greatest when:
facts.truth: they were unemployed or both partners worked full time
distractors: they had rediscovered themselves as 'new men'
their wives were at home and nagged them all the time
exciting gadgets like the hoover and electric iron were invented

facts.context: The shift from custodial to joint parenthood after divorce means that:
facts.truth: both parents are expected to co-operate in the continued care of their children
distractors: fathers are more likely to be absent from their children's lives
one parent has total responsibility for the child's welfare and socialization
parents must provide for all of their children in equal measure

facts.context: Émile Durkheim believed the primary focus of sociology should be which of the following?
facts.truth: Social facts
distractors: Social action
Class relations
Cultural dynamics

facts.context: The term "sociology" was coined in the early 1800s by which of the following?
facts.truth: Auguste Comte
distractors: Robert K. Merton
Karl Marx
Emile Durkheim

facts.context: In their study of depression, Brown & Harris (1978) found that:
facts.truth: women's susceptibility to stress depended on their levels of social support
distractors: self-reported depression is highest amongst men and the relatively affluent
men and women have fairly similar levels of diagnosed depression
men are more vulnerable to depression because of conventional gender roles

facts.context: The alternative form of 'network organization' means that:
facts.truth: work can be subcontracted out to independent suppliers and retailers
distractors: business transactions occur only through electronic communication
the Japanese model is applied, through lateral networks of flexible roles
activities are redistributed equally between men and women

facts.context: Margaret Mead suggested that:
facts.truth: adolescence and gender roles varied between societies and so were culturally determined
distractors: gender roles in three New Guinea societies were identical and so must be biologically determined
adolescence in the USA is a time of relative calm compared to the experience in Japan and Europe
anthropological fieldwork can be problematic because the researcher's values affect the way they interpret their observations

facts.context: The term 'secondary deviation' refers to:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: the punishment or stigmatization of deviant acts
the labelling of an act as deviant through social reactions to it
the ways in which taking on a deviant role affects future action

facts.context: The effect of the Internet upon the public sphere has been to:
facts.truth: revive it, by reaffirming a commitment to freedom of speech
distractors: repress it, by promoting only the interests of elite groups
reproduce it, by emphasizing face-to-face contact with peer groups
replace it with a superior form of communication

facts.context: Weber said that the 'spirit of capitalism' could be traced back to:
facts.truth: Calvinists who engaged in ascetic practices to gain signs of salvation
distractors: the movement towards religious pluralism
inspirational Protestant groups who revived religious ideas
new religious movements who rejected traditional forms of labour

facts.context: The terms 'crisis of the 1970s' is used to refer to:
facts.truth: declining profits and rising unemployment
distractors: the eradication of the welfare state
rising divorce rates and the decline of the traditional family
an unfortunate twist in fashion sensibility

facts.context: The Macpherson Report highlighted the increasing problem of:
facts.truth: institutional racism: systematic forms of disadvantage built into the routines and practices of social institutions
distractors: hate crimes: violence against ethnic minorities simply because of their race
overt racism in the police force that must be tackled by institutional reforms
growing numbers of asylum seekers in need of long term accommodation

facts.context: Seven people standing silently while waiting for a bus at a bus stop is an example of which of the following?
facts.truth: An aggregate
distractors: A primary group
A secondary group
A social category

facts.context: Environmentalist social movements are global in the sense that:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: they increase our awareness of risks that affect the whole planet
they appeal to universal values and human rights
they use global media to generate publicity

facts.context: Chodorow (1978) argued that gender socialization occurred through:
facts.truth: both boys and girls being closely attached to their mothers, but then boys breaking away
distractors: girls being attached to their mothers but then breaking away
boys being attached to their fathers and girls to their mothers
both boys and girls being closely attached to their fathers, but girls breaking away

facts.context: The 'new man' of the 1980s was alleged to be:
facts.truth: sensitive, caring, and emotional
distractors: laddish, aggressive, and violent
a strong and dependable breadwinner
openly bisexual and proud of it

facts.context: In the 1960s, comprehensive schools did not eradicate class inequalities in education because:
facts.truth: selective schools could still 'cream off' the most able pupils, while the comprehensives put children into ability streams
distractors: they tended to recruit pupils from the inner cities and traditionally working class areas
teachers in comprehensive schools used the restricted code in the classroom, whereas working class pupils used elaborated codes of speech
national testing and league tables made the new schools highly competitive and selective

facts.context: In idealized views of science, the experimental method is said to involve:
facts.truth: isolating and measuring the effect of one variable upon another
distractors: testing out new research methods to see which one works best
using personal beliefs and values to decide what to study
interpreting data subjectively, drawing on theoretical paradigms

facts.context: An 'open' society is one that:
facts.truth: allows people to move between levels of the hierarchy
distractors: grants every member equal status
does not have any official secrets in its government
has permissive attitudes towards sexual behaviour

facts.context: The Nation of Islam group appealed to:
facts.truth: African-Americans who felt excluded from the 'ethnic melting pot' in the USA
distractors: British-born second generation immigrants from the Asian subcontinent
White Americans who wanted to convert to Islam
African-Caribbeans who lived in the inner cities and had a distinctive youth culture

facts.context: Comte's term 'positivism' refers to:
facts.truth: the precise, scientific study of observable phenomena
distractors: a theory that emphasizes the positive aspects of society
a theory that posits difficult questions and sets out to answer them
an unscientific set of laws about social progress

facts.context: Theories of racialized discourse suggest that:
facts.truth: the idea of race is socially constructed through powerful ideologies
distractors: race is an objective way of categorizing people on biological grounds
race relations in Britain and America can be traced back to colonial times
people choose their racial identity and this becomes fixed

facts.context: A major car accident occurs. One man stops his car and starts helping people. After seeing him stop, others also stop and start helping. Which of the following theories best explains the scenario?
facts.truth: Emergent-norm
distractors: Convergence
Contagion
Value-added

facts.context: A new highway extends from a major city though an area used for farmland. Which of the following is most likely to happen as a result?
facts.truth: Suburbanization will develop because the time it takes to commute to the city has been decreased.
distractors: Farmers will increase the acreage under production.
The city will evolve into a multiple nuclei model.
People from the countryside will move to the city, depopulating the farmland region.

facts.context: According to W. E. B. Dubois, progress for African Americans in the aftermath of slavery
facts.truth: depended on the small group of African Americans receiving the same thorough and extensive education as the most talented White Americans were able to access
distractors: involved their use of skills they already had from farming, domestic work, and labors of rural life rather than being forced to learn new skills in the late 1800s
required that they be trained and prepared as craftspeople and service employees so they could become immediately self-sufficient
required them to move to large cities where they would be accepted as equals in government, religion, and education

facts.context: Pluralist theories suggest that:
facts.truth: political parties must compete for the votes of 'consumers' in the electorate
distractors: the state's power can be exercised through several different administrative structures
the ruling elite is composed of people from various class backgrounds
there is a close alignment between class background and party preference

facts.context: The term 'over-urbanization' means that:
facts.truth: in poorer countries, the rapidly developing cities drain resources from the rural areas
distractors: life in modern Western cities is so far removed from that of the Third World that we find it difficult to understand these societies
the extent to which urbanization affects development has been exaggerated
governments are so preoccupied with urbanization in the West that they forget to attend to problems in the Third World

facts.context: The capitalist class of the mid-twentieth century were said to join the upper class because they:
facts.truth: owned companies and financial assets that generated wealth through corporations
distractors: participated in the same leisure pursuits and events of the 'social calendar'
emulated the lifestyle and cultural values of the traditional aristocracy
had direct, personal ownerships of land and businesses as physical assets

facts.context: The term inter-generational mobility refers to:
facts.truth: movement into different occupational categories between generations
distractors: movement into a different occupational category over a person's lifetime
movement into a higher occupational category
movement into an occupation that generates a lower income

facts.context: The term 'culture industry', used by members of the Frankfurt School, referred to:
facts.truth: the way in which cultural products were bought and sold for profit
distractors: the globalization of culture through new technologies
the development of subcultures and counter-cultures in society
the way in which industrialization had created new means of communication

facts.context: According to Hakim (1995, 1996), the majority of part-time work is done by women because:
facts.truth: they make a rational choice to combine employment with their 'home-centred' lives
distractors: part-time jobs pay relatively high wages and so are financially beneficial
their choices are constrained by structural factors, such as being unable to afford childcare
full time work is not available in the kinds of occupations that they want to enter

facts.context: A sect is organised around:
facts.truth: voluntary membership, democratic leadership, and high levels of emotional commitment
distractors: beliefs that can be challenged by members, a charismatic leader, and openness to new recruits
tolerance towards other religions and the separation of church and state
the idea of the secular world itself as being sacred

facts.context: What is the name of the process by which we acquire a sense of identity and become members of society?
facts.truth: socialization
distractors: rationalization
colonization
McDonaldization

facts.context: Pre-testing a questionnaire is useful for:
facts.truth: identifying any problems with the question wording and routing
distractors: finding out which topics are important to the respondents
testing out your computer and printer
deciding which respondents to leave out of the final sample

facts.context: Judith Butler (1999) suggested that:
facts.truth: gender is performed through bodily gestures and styles to create 'sex'
distractors: sexual characteristics are the biological determinants of gender
heterosexuality and homosexuality are essential, opposing identities
the 'two-sex' model replaced the 'one-sex' model in the eighteenth century

facts.context: Patterns of drug use in Britain reveal that:
facts.truth: it has increased and is no longer confined to a hedonistic youth subculture
distractors: the most commonly used recreational drug is Ecstasy
teenage girls are more likely to experiment with hard drugs than teenage boys
despite a series of moral panics, the use of illegal drugs is very rare

facts.context: Murray thought that the 'underclass' consisted of people who:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: formed an inferior 'race' with low levels of intelligence
lived morally unsound lives of crime and squalor
were too reliant upon welfare benefits

facts.context: Religious organizations such as the Church of Norway, Islam, the Church of England, and the Church of Greece have which of the following characteristics in common?
facts.truth: They are monotheistic.
distractors: They are cultures as well as churches.
They practice separation of church and state.
They exclude women as clergy.

facts.context: A researcher mailed 1,000 questionnaires to members of a labor union and received 300 back. Most of those who returned the questionnaires indicated that they were dissatisfied with the union. Results based only on the returned questionnaires are most likely contaminated by
facts.truth: response rate bias
distractors: researcher bias
confusing correlation and causation
statistical regression

facts.context: Role-learning theory suggests that
facts.truth: we internalise and take on social roles from a pre-existing framework
distractors: we create and negotiate our roles through interaction with others
social roles are not fixed or stable but fluid and pluralistic
roles have to be learned to suppress unconscious motivations

facts.context: Post-modernist writers have argued that:
facts.truth: we live in a world of superficial, fragmented images
distractors: no theory is better than any other: 'anything goes'
society has changed and we need new kinds of theory
all of the above

facts.context: Leroy visits his parents, who live in a primarily African American working-class neighborhood. During his visit, he notices that the community is changing. Several developments have been built to accommodate single-family homes, and upscale shopping centers have been added. While Leroy appreciates these changes, he is worried that his parents will not be able to afford to continue living in their neighborhood because property values are rising. Which of the following best explains what is happening to Leroy's old neighborhood?
facts.truth: Gentrification
distractors: Urbanization
White flight
The demographic transition

facts.context: Warner's study of the city of Natchez in the American Deep South showed that the 'colour line' there was:
facts.truth: a sharp division between black and white castes, underpinned by beliefs about white superiority
distractors: no longer a powerful influence upon race relations
a way of marking out the land so that black people had less space for housing
a new policy that had been introduced to assimilate black and white groups

facts.context: The social construction of childhood can be traced back to:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: the introduction of compulsory education
increasingly emotional ties between parents and children
new consumer goods for children, such as clothes, toys and books

facts.context: In Marx's theory, the 'mode of production' means:
facts.truth: the organization of a society's technical and human resources
distractors: the way in which products are made in a factory
the average measure of productivity under capitalism
an integral part of the superstructure of a society

facts.context: The trend of decarceration involves:
facts.truth: community alternatives to imprisonment and institutional care
distractors: the increased use of imprisonment as the main form of punishment
transferring the management of prisons to private companies
decentralized organization due to the mobility of capital

facts.context: The work of Smart suggested that:
facts.truth: women's criminal behaviour tended to reflect traditional gender roles
distractors: the rates of violent crime were similar for men and women
women's sexual delinquency was more likely to be normalized than men's
all of the above

facts.context: Allen's (2001) study of nurses showed that:
facts.truth: the role of the nurse was ambiguous and had to be constantly redefined in the context of everyday interactions with other staff and patients
distractors: nurses perform a great deal of emotional labour for which they are not paid
the policies of Project 2000 had the effect of de-professionalising the career of nursing
more men were moving into nursing, which challenges some of the prevailing stereotypes about the occupation

facts.context: Which of the following best describes the practice of placing the blame for the problems of a society on one particular group?
facts.truth: Scapegoating
distractors: Ethnocentrism
Stereotyping
Institutionalization

facts.context: Dahrendorf, Rex, and Habermas focused their attention on:
facts.truth: power, domination, and conflict
distractors: social solidarity and cohesion
the interpretive understanding of action
women's experiences and gendered knowledge

facts.context: Sreberny-Mohammadi (1996) argues that national cultures can resist American cultural domination of the media by:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: domesticating its content, including more 'home-produced' programmes
controlling the distribution of imported products by banning satellite dishes
creating 'reverse flows' of their own programmes back to imperial societies

facts.context: Mulholland (1998) argued that privatization changed the relationship between companies and managers in that:
facts.truth: employment depended on performance rather than trust and commitment
distractors: flexibility was reduced by the introduction of detailed daily worksheets
the state had greater control than managers over production processes
ownership was transferred from small shareholders to senior managers

facts.context: Which of the following is consistent with results of current research conducted in the area of gender roles and the workplace?
facts.truth: Most men married to working women do not share housework and childcare obligations equally with their working wives.
distractors: In about a quarter of households with children, men stay home with the children so their wives can work.
Most women believe that there is equal opportunity today between men and women in career choice, advancement, and income.
Most men believe that women should be homemakers because men earn more money.

facts.context: Socialization is:
facts.truth: the process of becoming part of a society by learning its norms and values
distractors: the formation of an attachment bond between an infant and its carer
a tendency of social theorists to explain everything in terms of social causes
the historical process by which societies change from traditional to modern

facts.context: The term 'assets' refers to:
facts.truth: a stock of economic resources, including land, shares and bank deposits
distractors: the culturally valued commodities and standards of living that make the poor feel relatively deprived
the flow of money a person receives from their salary or wage
the 'slices' of the population who own differing amounts of wealth

facts.context: In the traditional hierarchy of status and precedence, which of these members of the upper class are in the right order (from highest to lowest status)?
facts.truth: King's Grandsons, Lord High Treasurer, Companions of the Bath
distractors: Prime Minister, Archbishop of York, Viscounts of England
Marquesses of England, Earls of Great Britain, King's Brothers
Esquires, Serjeants of Law, Dukes' Eldest Sons

facts.context: Social norms are:
facts.truth: rules and expectations about interaction that regulate social life
distractors: creative activities such as gardening, cookery and craftwork
the symbolic representation of social groups in the mass media
religious beliefs about how the world ought to be

facts.context: The 'nuclear family' means:
facts.truth: a two generation unit of parents and their children
distractors: a group of people sharing living accommodation and meals
a network of relatives extended within or between generations
the new family created when an adult leaves home and gets married

facts.context: The rise of new social movements indicates that:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: people vote according to pragmatic issues and concerns
consumer identities are more important than class identities
personal, cultural and environmental issues can mobilise political consciousness just as much as economic interests

facts.context: Which of the following sociological perspectives views society as a system of interdependent and coordinated parts?
facts.truth: Functionalism
distractors: Conflict theory
Symbolic interactionism
Role theory

facts.context: Post-Fordist forms of media production and consumption involve:
facts.truth: a diverse range of products aimed at niche markets
distractors: the mass production of standardized products for passive audiences
television based on producer-broadcaster rather than publisher-broadcaster models
increasing numbers of advertisements for motoring and car-related products

facts.context: Marx said that the development of the labour movement through factory-based production would turn the working class into:
facts.truth: a class for itself
distractors: a class in itself
a class by itself
a ruling class

facts.context: In stage 3 of the 'health transition', the main causes of illness and death are:
facts.truth: chronic, degenerative diseases, such as cancer, heart disease, and strokes
distractors: acute, infectious diseases such as typhus, measles and cholera
respiratory diseases such as pneumonia and asthma
too diverse to put into one category

facts.context: Sociologists like Bordo (1993) have attributed the 'epidemic' of anorexia in Western cultures to:
facts.truth: the new ideal of independent, self-assured femininity, combined with cultural values of achievement and control
distractors: a generally ascetic, frugal lifestyle that can be taken to extremes
traditional images of women as emotional, domesticated, and unassertive
the social construction of mental illness, through which 'normal' patterns of behaviour are pathologized

facts.context: One of the difficulties in 'operationalizing' concepts like social class is that:
facts.truth: definitions and indicators can vary, making valid comparisons problematic
distractors: there are no reliable indicators of such widely contested ideas
it takes all the fun out of armchair theorising
it has little use for applied, empirical research about the topic

facts.context: In the 1990 reform of the National Health Service, hospitals became:
facts.truth: self-governing trusts competing for purchasing contracts from health authorities
distractors: state-controlled providers, dependent on funding from the central government
increasingly detached from health authorities and providers of private health care
less inclined to run themselves efficiently, as demand for health care was falling

facts.context: Law and order was privatized in the 1980s and 1990s through:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: the expansion of private security companies
the building of private prisons
the introduction of market disciplines to measure police performance

facts.context: The 'correspondence principle' (Bowles & Gintis) suggests that:
facts.truth: schools prepare children for work by teaching them to be obedient
distractors: teachers and parents tend to have similar attitudes to learning
children who write lots of letters develop a better grasp of language
boys' and girls' educational achievements have recently become similar

facts.context: Class segregation was apparent in mid-twentieth century Britain insofar as:
facts.truth: working class communities formed in the inner cities, while the middle classes moved out to the suburbs
distractors: middle class owner-occupiers moved into the inner city through gentrification
the middle classes lived in the countryside while the working class lived in the city
the upper and middle classes dominated public space, while working class people were more home-centred

facts.context: Charles Cooley's theory of the looking-glass self emphasizes
facts.truth: how one's self-concept is based on one's perception of how others see one
distractors: the manner in which young children come to a realization that they have a separate identity
the difficulty one experiences in seeing oneself as others do
how one's self-concept is heavily influenced by one's physical appearance

facts.context: Queer Theory makes the claim that:
facts.truth: all sexualities are pluralistic, fragmented and frequently reconstructed
distractors: heterosexuality is the normal and most desirable way to be
the sexual categories and discourses we use are based upon true, underlying biological differences
deviant forms of masculinity are seen as more threatening to the gender order than deviant forms of femininity

facts.context: Scott (1991) introduced the term 'power elite' to describe:
facts.truth: a state elite whose members are drawn overwhelmingly from a power bloc
distractors: the ruling class, or bourgeoisie, who exploit the proletariat
a capitalist class dependent on property ownership and advantaged life chances
an alignment of classes with shared interests but no state power

facts.context: A sociologist is conducting research on institutional power relations at a local hospital. The sociologist is on staff at the hospital for several weeks, recording data as field notes. This method of research is called
facts.truth: participant observation
distractors: experimentation
secondary data analysis
survey administration

facts.context: In Symbolic Interactionist theory, Mead defined the 'generalized other' as:
facts.truth: an image of how people in the wider society might perceive our behaviour
distractors: the group of structural theories of society that he was reacting against
the overall impression of ourselves that we try to give off to others
a significant figure in early childhood who teaches us the general values of society

facts.context: According to Davis (1995), women may seek cosmetic surgery because:
facts.truth: they perceive a part of their body as stigmatizing, in relation to a cultural ideal
distractors: they have a deep-rooted sense of ineffectiveness and are striving for control
they are in pursuit of the 'perfect' body, fuelled by images of beauty
their male partners pressurize them to look like supermodels

facts.context: Marx (1844) claimed that capitalism had alienated workers from:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: the product they were making
the production process
each other and humanity in general

facts.context: Herberg's (1955) study of religion in America suggested that:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: ethnic minorities practised religion to achieve social acceptance in the culture
mainstream faiths were becoming increasingly identified with national identity
the moral teachings of the main religions were becoming relatively similar

facts.context: Structural-Functionalists describe society as:
facts.truth: a normative framework of roles and institutions
distractors: a complex network of interaction at a micro-level
a source of conflict, inequality, and alienation
an unstable structure of social relations

facts.context: Booth's (1901) study revealed that the percentage of people in London who were living in poverty was:
Traditional working class identity was based around:
facts.truth: local communities, extended kinship networks and shared leisure pursuits
distractors: shared working conditions in the manufacturing industry
the class consciousness of members of the proletariat
collective aspirations to move into the middle class

facts.context: Bernstein thought that using 'restricted codes' of language disadvantaged pupils at school because:
facts.truth: they involved short, simple sentences with a small vocabulary
distractors: this pattern of speech made them the target of bullying
they referred to explicit, context independent meanings
they prevented children from communicating outside of their peer groups

facts.context: Telework is a form of employment that involves:
facts.truth: working at home for an outside employer, using information technology
distractors: running one's own business through telephone helplines or hotlines
babysitting and other jobs within a local network, through the informal economy
combining non-manual work with the leisure activity of watching television

facts.context: Frank (1967) made the claim that 'underdeveloped' societies were:
facts.truth: economically dependent on the wealthy countries that exploited them
distractors: insufficiently involved in the international capitalist economy
reluctant to surrender their traditional ways of life
the 'metropoles' to which 'satellite' countries were attached

facts.context: The case of the former Soviet Union supports Kerr et al.'s (1960) claim that:
facts.truth: totalitarian regimes are incompatible with the requirements of advanced industrial technology
distractors: democratic governments serve the interests of a ruling elite
advanced industrial technology strengthens the power of totalitarian regimes
communist societies disperse power between various political parties

facts.context: The print revolution of the nineteenth century resulted in:
facts.truth: mass production, mass circulation, and the decline of serious content
distractors: the public ownership of newspapers as a shared resource
more people going to the cinema as a new leisure activity
a reduction of 'virtual communities' on the Internet

facts.context: The term 'feminist standpoint' suggests:
facts.truth: studying society from the perspective of women
distractors: taking a stand on the issues neglected by feminism
the recognition of difference and diversity in women's lives
a tendency to ignore the gendered nature of knowledge

facts.context: Ethnographic research produces qualitative data because:
facts.truth: it uncovers rich, detailed accounts from an insider's perspective
distractors: the findings are amenable to statistical analysis
it is conducted over a period of several years
it compares findings from a number of different cases

facts.context: Marx proclaimed that religion would disappear when:
facts.truth: a socialist revolution removed the need for capitalist ideology
distractors: a new trend of moral individualism became established
church attendance dropped below 15%
people came to believe more in science and technology

facts.context: Which of the following is not a feature of globalization?
facts.truth: the extended power of nation states
distractors: an increasing awareness of the world as a whole
the destruction of distance through communications technologies
the stretching of social relations beyond national boundaries

facts.context: Someone who gains a higher (or lower) social position than he or she was born into has experienced
facts.truth: vertical mobility
distractors: absolute poverty
relative poverty
horizontal mobility

facts.context: A survey should avoid asking:
facts.truth: leading questions
distractors: fixed-choice questions
short questions
funnelled questions

facts.context: The theory of development which suggests that societies move from traditional to modern, industrial forms of organization is called:
facts.truth: Modernization theory
distractors: Westernization theory
Industrialization theory
Dependency theory

facts.context: In modern societies, social status is typically measured by a person's:
facts.truth: occupation
distractors: age
income
verbal fluency

facts.context: Sociology differs from common sense in that:
facts.truth: its knowledge is accumulated from many different research contexts
distractors: it focuses on the researchers' own experiences
it makes little distinction between the way the world is and the way it ought to be
it is subjective and biased

facts.context: World-affirming religions:
facts.truth: embrace conventional cultural values but offer new means of achieving them
distractors: react against the loss of any meaningful religious content in the teachings of churches
adopt an attitude of mild disapproval towards mainstream social values
reject both the goals and means of conventional society and provide utopian alternatives

facts.context: The 'extension pattern' that Parker (1976) identified was said to be:
facts.truth: characteristic of professionals and skilled workers, whose work interests spilled over into leisure time
distractors: the lifestyle of clerical and unskilled manual workers, who saw leisure time as a chance to escape from alienating work
a neutral balance between work and leisure as neither connected nor oppositional, as evidenced by semi-skilled manual and clerical workers
the tendency of students to leave essay-writing until the last minute and then ask for an extended deadline

facts.context: Weber defined a 'class situation' as:
facts.truth: a person's position in the capital, product and labour markets, based on their economic resources
distractors: the exploitation of the working class by their capitalist employers
a social group's consciousness of their status and life chances
the lifestyle of a social class, as defined by patterns of consumption

facts.context: The introduction of market principles to educational policy in the 1980s resulted in:
facts.truth: increased state regulation through national testing and inspections
distractors: more funding for students in higher education
the delegation of power and budgetary control to LEAs
a reduction in parental choice of school

facts.context: Smith & Tomlinson argued that:
facts.truth: school character far outweighed ethnic background in determining educational success
distractors: differences in ethnic background were more significant than class or gender in predicting educational achievement
racism in schools undermined the move towards equality of opportunity for children from ethnic minorites
gender differences in educational achievement could be attributed to inequalities of class

facts.context: The 'post-industrial society' identified by Bell (1973) involved:
facts.truth: a shift towards a service economy, based on knowledge and information
distractors: the increasing power of trade unions in resolving industrial disputes
a resurgence of interest in industrial production and manufacturing
all of the above

facts.context: White-collar crime is low in visibility because:
facts.truth: it goes undetected in the context of everyday business transactions
distractors: it involves only small amounts of money
the proletariat can outsmart the bourgeoisie
the police turn a blind eye to corporate crime

facts.context: With industrial capitalism, leisure emerged as a concept that meant:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: distinct periods of non-work and relaxation
regulated and organized activities to reduce social disorder
the growth of civic, municipal culture through public libraries, museums and art galleries

facts.context: A moral panic occurs when:
facts.truth: the media exaggerate reports of deviant groups, generating hostile reactions
distractors: children watch violent or sexually explicit films and then copy the behaviour
audiences challenge the ethnic stereotypes represented by the media
elitists express concern with the Americanization of culture

facts.context: The 'demographic transition' is a social trend that involves:
facts.truth: a decline in the birth rate, greater life expectancy, and an ageing population
distractors: a reduction in population size, caused by a higher rate of emigration than immigration
a change in the principal causes of death and disease since industrialization
increased birth and death rates, resulting in a relatively young population

facts.context: 'Snowballing' is an example of:
facts.truth: non-probability sampling
distractors: probability sampling
cluster sampling
using the Christmas vacation constructively

facts.context: The 'class polarization' that Marx predicted referred to:
facts.truth: a growing gap between rich and poor, resulting in class consciousness
distractors: the division of each social class into the more or less privileged
the growth of intermediate strata in the middle classes
the tendency for the working class to live in very cold places

facts.context: In the context of the labour movement in the nineteenth century, 'incorporation' meant:
facts.truth: including working class organizations in political bargaining and representation
distractors: including union representatives in processes of policy decision making
creating links between the state and corporate organizations
recruiting women into full time paid employment

facts.context: Millions of immigrant children who entered the United States learned English and the duties of citizenship while attending public schools in the communities in which they settled. This is an example of which of the following sociological concepts?
facts.truth: Acculturation
distractors: Collective behavior
Social stratification
Social mobility

facts.context: The capitalist world economy is what Wallerstein (1974) would call a 'world system'. This term refers to:
facts.truth: a unit with a division of labour that extends across ethnic and cultural groups
distractors: a means of transporting money between different areas of a country
an empire with a bureaucratic administration but no political centre
an awareness of risks and dangers that affect the environment as a whole

facts.context: According to Foucault, carceral organizations:
facts.truth: isolated people and put them under surveillance
distractors: emphasised human relations and employee satisfaction
increased the amount of power held by managers
stripped inmates of their previous identities

facts.context: Which of the following is not recognised as a level of society?
facts.truth: the office
distractors: the household
the global village
the nation state

facts.context: Ethnic identity refers to:
facts.truth: a felt sense of group membership on the basis of religion, language, or history
distractors: the objective categories of ethnicity used in the census
the imposition of a racial label on a minority by a powerful majority
the fragmented and pluralistic nature of ethnicity

facts.context: Marriage appears to be in decline because:
facts.truth: many people are cohabiting in long term relationships
distractors: the proportion of people living alone has fallen to 29%
the upward curve of remarriages compensates for the drop in first marriages
all of the above

facts.context: The market model of state welfare is based on the principle of:
facts.truth: individuals buying welfare privately, with some means-tested benefits
distractors: regular benefit payments to men as earners of the 'family wage'
a universalist system of welfare for all, regardless of income
decommodifying social welfare through state provision

facts.context: The term feminization of poverty refers to:
facts.truth: women's increased chances of being in poverty, due to low pay and greater welfare dependency
distractors: the critical deconstruction of 'poverty' by feminist theorists
the way in which managing a budget and avoiding poverty tends to be a woman's responsibility within the home
the disproportionate number of female sociologists who do research on poverty

facts.context: Which of the following is not identified as a new form of community?
facts.truth: sociological communities, formed by unpopular lecturers
distractors: ethnic communities, based on shared identity and experiences of discrimination
gay villages, which are formed in certain parts of large cities
virtual communities that exist only in cyberspace

facts.context: The use of 'indentured labour' in the nineteenth century involved:
facts.truth: people being transported to the British colonies and forced to work for one employer under poor conditions
distractors: the sale of African people through the slave trade in the 'Atlantic triangle'
selecting the most skilled workers in factories to promote their employer's company overseas
extracting the teeth of the laziest workers to stop them from talking

facts.context: Cultural restructuring has involved:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: regenerating cities in economic decline
turning industrial landscapes into tourist attractions
selling sites and images through the 'symbolic economy' of media advertising

facts.context: Which of the following was not part of Weber's ideal type of bureaucracy?
facts.truth: extensive paperwork
distractors: impersonal rules
hierarchy of officials
Salaries

facts.context: Which of the following is the most important agent of socialization in early childhood?
facts.truth: The family
distractors: A preschool provider
A peer group
The mass media

facts.context: Which of the following is not a 'research purpose'?
facts.truth: triangulation
distractors: explanation
description
exploration

facts.context: 'Scientific' theories in the nineteenth century tried to explain race in naturalistic terms. Which of the following ideas was not considered?
facts.truth: height
distractors: genetics
evolution
brain size

facts.context: Which of the following is not a regular national survey carried out by the British government?
facts.truth: Fashion Sensibility Survey
distractors: Labour Force Survey
General Household Survey
Family Expenditure Survey

facts.context: Which of the following industries did Blauner (1964) suggest was most alienating to its employees?
facts.truth: car manufacturing in assembly plants
distractors: machine-based factory work
textile work as craft production
chemical processing with automated practices

facts.context: The 'decentralized city' can be identified by:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: the shift of employment and services away from the inner city towards multiple centres in smaller towns and rural areas
the degendering of public space as women use local facilities more
gentrification: the movement of middle class people back into the inner city

facts.context: Lombroso claimed that:
facts.truth: biological failings drove some people into crime
distractors: criminals were socialized into an 'underworld' of crime
no act is intrinsically deviant
women were less likely to be arrested than men

facts.context: Warner and Myrdal claimed that black former slaves were not joining the 'ethnic melting pot' of the North American cities because:
facts.truth: the promise of citizenship was contradicted by continued discrimination
distractors: the white population did not believe in liberty, equality, and democracy
they wanted to retain a strong sense of their original ethnic identity
they were not prepared to leave the Southern states and move to North America

facts.context: When Berger & Luckmann said that reality is socially constructed, they meant:
facts.truth: people negotiate shared definitions of their situation and live according to these, often forgetting that these social worlds are not fixed and external
distractors: scientists are guided in their work by social values and interests, so they define and measure phenomena that will support their theories
sociologists decide what constitutes social reality and measure only that
terms like 'reality' have no deeper meaning beyond the level of discourse

facts.context: Which of the following groups or organizations can be identified as having the characteristics of a bureaucracy?
facts.truth: The government of a large city
distractors: An extended or consanguine family, including all the relatives by blood, marriage, or adoption
A book club in which books are chosen by members who take turns alphabetically to lead discussion
The volunteers who gather annually to clean the neighborhood

facts.context: Pilcher (1999) identified soap operas as a 'feminine genre' of media because:
facts.truth: they represent images of women as both domesticated and independent
distractors: most of the characters in soap operas are women
they alienate women and appeal to an audience of men
female television producers are most likely to work in this area

facts.context: Which of these was not one of Townsend's (1979) indicators of relative deprivation?
facts.truth: buying fewer than twenty DVDs in the previous year
distractors: lacking exclusive use of a bath or shower
living in housing with serious structural defects
going without a week's holiday in the previous year

facts.context: Children who are still sucking their thumbs when they enter kindergarten are often subject to teasing and ridicule for this behavior. What type of social sanction is applied in this instance?
facts.truth: Informal negative sanction
distractors: Formal positive sanction
Formal negative sanction
Informal positive sanction

facts.context: The concept of political culture refers to the
facts.truth: basic beliefs and orientation toward politics that are shared widely by the members of a culture
distractors: way that cultural products, such as novels or paintings, reflect the political ideologies of their creators
political beliefs and attitudes shared by people in public offices
ideological biases of the mass media

facts.context: Walt Disney, Sony and Time Warner are examples of:
facts.truth: transnational corporations
distractors: multi-media empires
ownership concentrated within one medium
government-owned companies

facts.context: Equilibrium theories of social change emphasize
facts.truth: stability and the structure of society
distractors: globalization
secularism and mechanization
industrialization

facts.context: Which of the following best describes the definition of poverty used by the United States government?
facts.truth: It specifies the poverty line at a level set in the 1960s and adjusted since to reflect inflation.
distractors: It includes the value of government services, such as Medicaid or public housing, when setting the poverty line.
It is based on the concept of relative deprivation, and the poverty line changes as the distribution of income changes.
It is based on the concept of lifestyle, and the poverty line varies according to the needs of the individual.

facts.context: The 'absolute' poverty line is drawn to show:
facts.truth: the estimated minimum level of income needed for subsistence
distractors: the most extreme level of poverty that is found in a society
households that are poor, relative to the norms and values of their culture
the areas of a city in which poverty is concentrated

facts.context: 'Eyeballing' is a technique that involves:
facts.truth: scanning a table to identify general patterns and significant figures
distractors: reading a textual document quickly to understand the gist of it
gathering a sample from whoever you can see in a public place
keeping your eyes on research participants at all times

facts.context: Which of the following was not a strategy used by the Conservative government of 1979 to reduce the power of the labour movement?
facts.truth: making all strike action illegal
distractors: imposing restrictions on picketing
banning political strikes that were not primarily concerned with the worker's own conditions of work
making secondary action (in support of workers elsewhere) illegal

facts.context: In the nineteenth century, homosexuality was understood as:
facts.truth: a subordinate form of masculinity that threatened 'compulsory heterosexuality'
distractors: a positive identity in which gay people could take pride
an absolute taboo, which meant that all homosexuals were isolated
confirmation of the two-sex model

facts.context: The population pyramid of a population that has had a slightly larger birth rate than death rate for several generations will most likely have which of the following shapes?
facts.truth: Wider at the bottom than at the top
distractors: Wider at the top than in the middle
Bulging in the middle
Having parallel sides

facts.context: The 'new racism' refers to:
facts.truth: a subtler form of prejudice, masked by nationalist pride
distractors: a post-modern deconstruction of racist ideas to reveal their lack of depth
racist practices found in newly emerging areas of social life, such as cyberspace
an anti-fascist movement which challenges nationalist politics

facts.context: Giselle graduated from college with high honors in management. She was recruited immediately following college by a large corporation and accepted a position in junior management. She quickly advanced to middle management. Ten years later Giselle was still in a middle-management position and had witnessed junior male colleagues promoted over her. Which of the following is a phenomenon that is most likely to explain Giselle's lack of promotion?
facts.truth: Glass ceiling
distractors: Gender constancy
Underemployment
Glass elevator

facts.context: The Mafia is an example of:
facts.truth: organized crime
distractors: white collar crime
non-criminal deviance
global terrorism

facts.context: Goldthorpe identified the 'service class' as:
facts.truth: those in non-manual occupations, exercising authority on behalf of the state
distractors: people working in consultancy firms who were recruited by big businesses
the young men and women employed in domestic service in the nineteenth century
those who had worked in the armed services

facts.context: Industrial production in the nineteenth century was characterized by:
facts.truth: power-driven machinery and the concentration of workers in large workplaces
distractors: increasing numbers of women in high status positions
cottage industries and homeworkers doing piecework in the domestic sphere
the 'managerial revolution' by which ownership and control became separate

facts.context: The term 'collective consumption' (Castells 1977) refers to:
facts.truth: the provision of health, housing, and education services by the state
distractors: the privatization of public services by the Conservative government
the lifestyle practice of shopping in peer groups
the form of tuberculosis suffered by those who collect stamps

facts.context: The deviant subculture of homosexuals who met in seventeenth and eighteenth century London was called the:
facts.truth: mollies
distractors: pollies
dollies
lollies

facts.context: Robert and Tanya are married and live with their three children. Their family unit is called
facts.truth: a nuclear family
distractors: a family of orientation
an extended family
a communal family

facts.context: In Durkheim's work, the term 'collective representations' refers to:
facts.truth: shared ideas and moral values, often symbolized by an object or figurehead
distractors: effervescent ceremonies that create a feeling of belonging
images of gods or totems that are widely recognized
ideological tools used to obscure class divisions

facts.context: Which of the following is not a characteristic of totalitarian societies?
facts.truth: freedom of movement for citizens
distractors: indoctrination and propaganda
one-party rule
a centrally planned economy

facts.context: Freud's notion of the 'ego' referred to:
facts.truth: the conscious part of the mind that regulates emotional drives on a practical, rational level
distractors: the unconscious mass of instinctive drives that may be repressed
the self as a whole: an unstable mix of conscious and unconscious elements
the neurotic part of the mind that longs for belonging and may suffer an inferiority complex

facts.context: Which of the following theories focuses on how society manages to carry out the activities necessary to maintain order and stability?
facts.truth: Functionalism
distractors: Interactionism
Rational-choice theory
Conflict theory

facts.context: Which of the following is a personal document with closed access?
facts.truth: household account book
distractors: government 'white paper'
confidential medical records
the shares register of a business

facts.context: The tripartite system used the 11+ exam to select pupils for:
facts.truth: grammar, technical, and secondary modern schools
distractors: popular schools that lay outside their catchment area
private, public and comprehensive schools
polytechnics, colleges and universities

facts.context: Durkheim defined social facts as:
facts.truth: ways of acting, thinking, and feeling that are collective and social in origin
distractors: the way scientists construct knowledge in a social context
data collected about social phenomena that are proven to be correct
ideas and theories that have no basis in the external, physical world

facts.context: The media-themes model of media influence claims that:
facts.truth: social interaction reinforces the ideas and images that audiences select
distractors: audiences selectively interpret what they want to hear
content analysis is the best way to identify the themes covered by the media
audiences passively absorb whatever messages they are given

facts.context: Bowlby's maternal deprivation thesis claimed that:
facts.truth: children deprived of an early, secure attachment to their mother are prone to suffer physically, intellectually and socially in later life
distractors: mothers who are living in poverty cannot afford to give their children the resources that other children enjoy
'mothering' is a socially constructed activity identified in the narratives of new mothers
deprivation is something children inherit, usually through their mother's side

facts.context: War became possible between nation states in the nineteenth century because:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: the world was divided into several rival overseas empires
industrialization provided better transport, technology and administration
there was intense economic, political and military competition

facts.context: Which of the following is not identified by Fulcher & Scott as a criterion of community?
facts.truth: a fixed geographical location
distractors: a shared sense of identity and belonging together
common activities involving all-round relationships
collective action based on common interests

facts.context: The 'Great Migration' of 1916-1919 meant that Chicago became an ethnically diverse city. The consequence of this was:
facts.truth: most ethnic groups were integrated into mainstream society, but the black population remained in the city's ghettos
distractors: assimilation: all racial boundaries were dissolved by the 'ethnic melting pot'
multiculturalism: all ethnic identities were celebrated equally
racism: the white host population were hostile and prejudiced towards migrants

facts.context: According to Scott (1990), a document is 'authentic' if it:
facts.truth: is a 'sound' original, or reliable copy, of known authorship
distractors: is a sincere and accurate account, free from political bias
is representative of all the similar documents that did not survive
has both a literal and an interpretable meaning

facts.context: Which of the following is not a consequence of global tourism?
facts.truth: decreased rates of prostitution and sex tourism
distractors: developing countries can depend on it as a crucial source of income
the exploitation of cheap, unregulated labour in poor countries
we have become more aware of 'other' societies and ways of living

facts.context: Which of the following did Thatcher's government not strive to achieve:
facts.truth: generous financial benefits for single parents, students and the unemployed
distractors: marketization and privatization
individual freedom, choice and responsibility
the rolling back of the welfare state

facts.context: One of the ethical problems with covert participant observation is:
facts.truth: deceiving the respondents as to the reason for your presence
distractors: not being able to write comprehensive fieldnotes or record conversations
observing people outside of their natural setting
subjecting the participants to physical harm

facts.context: The embourgeoisement thesis suggests that:
facts.truth: the affluent working class is adopting middle class values and ways of life
distractors: capitalists are being replaced by salaried managers with bureaucratic authority
white collar occupations are being 'deskilled' by the introduction of new technologies into the workplace
the class structure has fragmented and there are no longer any distinct social classes

facts.context: Women have been excluded from the public sphere because:
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: industrial capitalism separated the middle class home from the workplace
those who enter paid employment have been 'sidelined' into particular fields
it is difficult to succeed in 'malestream' politics without compromising their femininity

facts.context: Economic aid has largely failed to promote modernization in the developing countries because:
facts.truth: debt repayments with interest can be greater than the amount of money received
distractors: there are no clearly defined projects into which the money can be directed
the United Nations has refused to call on rich countries to provide it
debt repayments with interest can be greater than the amount of money received

facts.context: How is terrorism different from the types of crime described by the Chicago School?
facts.truth: all of the above
distractors: it is committed on a larger, often global, scale, and is well organized
it is associated with political conflict between states and their citizens
it can have far-reaching effects upon international relations

facts.context: The human relations approach emphasized the importance of:
facts.truth: teamwork, communication and employee satisfaction
distractors: scrutinizing employees as they interacted with customers
strict control and discipline to ensure high productivity
providing therapy and counselling in the workplace

facts.context: Which of the following did Domhoff not identify as a process of decision-making in the USA?
facts.truth: the exploitation process
distractors: the ideology process
the policy-formation process
the candidate-selection process
